figures.gloss: Òrìṣà of thunder and lightning — a deified king who hurls thunderstones from the sky.
figures.facet: A god who was once a human king: divinity earned by rule and remembered in storm.

figures.gloss: Òrìṣà of the crossroads and divine messenger — no prayer or offering reaches the gods except through him.
figures.facet: Not a rogue but a required gate: the trickster as the indispensable translator between human and divine.

figures.gloss: Òrìṣà of iron, war and the clearing of the path — patron of every craft that bites with metal.
figures.facet: The smith who is also the road-opener: iron as the tool that cuts the way into the future.

edges.note: Messenger of the crossroads who alone can carry words between human and divine.
Two West African tricksters, neighbours but unrelated in language-family — Esu the Yoruba crossroads-gate, Anansi the Akan spider who buys the stories. Functional kin, not cognates.
West African thunder/war powers in neighbouring but distinct traditions — Sango the Yoruba storm-king, Tano the Akan river-thunder. Structural kin, never cognate.
GOLD SEAM: Ambiguous, hyper-kinetic trickster spirits who master the crossroads of transformation, without whose compliance no ritual work can succeed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fierce, martial deities of raw will who cut away human weakness and clear the unyielding path for divine law to manifest.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Militant, dynamic patron deities associated with fire, storms, and royal authority.
The forest-lord as origin of the forge and iron-craft — the wild source of metallurgy, paralleling the orisa of iron and the smith-archetype.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba trickster and messenger, retaining his absolute role as the opener of paths, adapted directly into the Brazilian diaspora.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba lord of iron and war transplanted directly, retaining his metallurgy, machete, and path-clearing kinetic energy.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba king of Oyo, perfectly preserved in Brazil as the royal wielder of lightning and absolute justice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Powerful, iron-wielding, martial figures associated with modern industry, labor, and the fierce protection of the oppressed.
GOLD SEAM: A patron of iron, weaponry, and brotherhoods, revered by both outlaws and the police who hunt them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Elevation to the divine sphere intimately connected with the lethal, electrifying strike of the sky god's lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are unnumbered, boundary-crossing trickster figures who sit at the crossroads of divination, permitting or confusing the reading.
The disorder-bringing trickster who is nonetheless indispensable to divination and the working of fate — but Ogo's mischief is a fall, where Esu's is a necessary gate.

The guardian of the threshold and crossroads who patrols the boundary between the human village and the wild — Aiyaṉār at the field's edge and Eṣù at the crossroads, both the keeper of the dangerous seam, reached independently.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: In Espiritismo Cruzado, Shango frequently appears at the mesa blanca as the syncretic leader of the African Commission of spirit guides.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historical and functional continuity of the phallic, crossroads trickster and divine messenger.
Fon Legba and Yoruba Esu/Elegba are near-identical crossroads-tricksters among neighbouring peoples — an attested shared cult, not a distant analogy.
Gu (Fon) and Ogun (Yoruba) are the same iron-and-war smith-god shared across the Fon–Yoruba neighbourhood — a documented regional cult.
Neighbouring Fon and Yoruba thunder-gods who hurl stone axes and judge the guilty by lightning — shared West-African storm-cult, near-mirror roles.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Black Man at the crossroads is a North American syncretism deeply informed by the Yoruba trickster Eshu, who governs thresholds, intersections, and the delivery of esoteric knowledge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both combine the blinding, localized power of the sky (sun/lightning) with martial, masculine dominance.
GOLD SEAM: West African thunder-powers in neighbouring but distinct traditions — Yoruba Ṣàngó the storm-king and Igbo Amadioha the thunder-justice with his white ram. Structural kin, never cognate.

GOLD SEAM — but FLAG: Yoruba Èṣù and Igbo Ekwensu are neighbouring West African trickster-powers, both of contention, reversal, and the bargain, and BOTH were mistranslated by missionaries as 'the Devil.' Neither is a Satan: the demonization is the colonial overlay, not the indigenous figure. Functional kin, not cognates.

A loose reach: the game-master who owns the wild animals faintly parallels the orisa-owner of a domain, but Inuit master-spirits are keepers of consent in the hunt, not personal gods — flag as speculative.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A trickster figure who mediates between the human and divine, possessing supreme spiritual authority despite a deceptive exterior.
Both the indispensable channel between humans and the gods — no message passes except through them; the mudang diverges as a possessed human medium rather than a divine gatekeeper-orisa.

Tricksters who are also the keepers of language and the crossroads of meaning — Iktómi who named all things, Èṣù who carries every word between human and divine; the trickster as the hinge of communication.
The thunder imagined as a living, glancing power of the sky whose touch marks and consecrates certain people; the Thunderbeings and Ṣàngó are independent personifications of the storm's holy charge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A civilizing hero and divine blacksmith who radically transforms human society through the revelation of ironworking.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are fierce, uncompromising martial deities associated with young men, wilderness, warfare, and unyielding aggression.
GOLD SEAM: The double-headed axe wielded as a primary, stylized emblem of divine authority, storm power, and ritual presence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as the essential liminal mediators who must be honored first to open the spiritual roads and permit human-divine communication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The patron of iron and smithing whose identity is inextricably linked to extreme violence and the foundation of state authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unpredictable, vice-loving trickster who acts as an indispensable, chaotic intermediary during ritual possession.
Tricksters who are also the principle of unpredictability the order requires — Coyote and Èṣù both keep the world from freezing into dead perfection by introducing chance and change.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, dynamic martial gods whose violent, untamed power is intimately linked to the preservation of royal authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct survival and continuous worship of the Yoruba thunder king, masked through Saint John the Baptist.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct survival of the Yoruba iron-god, syncretized dynamically with St. Michael.
Trickster as indispensable mediator: Esu the required crossroads-gate, Raven the agent who alone moves between sealed cosmic boxes and the lit world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly syncretized in Cuba; Lucero acts as the Palo equivalent of the Yoruba crossroads opener.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ogun and Zarabanda are explicitly equated in Cuban practice as the uncompromising lords of iron, war, and blood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deeply conflated in Cuba, both ruling lightning, fire, and a fiery, sorcerous masculine kingship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The pipe-smoking trickster of the crossroads who mediates between the divine and the profane through commerce, loopholes, and humor.
GOLD SEAM: Restless, pioneering deities of masculine expansion, who clear the path for civilization, industry, and the violent expansion of empires.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Derives its core trickster and crossroads identity from the Yoruba Eshu, but alchemized into a supreme agent of left-hand sorcery.

Both are African tricksters at the seam between human and divine, but Esu is the required gate of sacrifice while |Kaggen is the maker himself in fool's-shape — the trickster as doorway versus the trickster as creator.
GOLD SEAM: A direct historical continuation of the Yoruba Eshu, surviving the Middle Passage by donning the masks of Catholic child-saints.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba king of thunder translated directly to Cuba, maintaining his dominion over drums, fire, and justice.
GOLD SEAM: The identical patron of iron, blood, and the wilderness, preserved with intense fidelity in the diaspora.
GOLD SEAM: Divinities of war and iron where the aniconic metal blade itself is the god and the direct receiver of blood sacrifice.
GOLD SEAM: The sacralization of iron and the martial arts; the belief that righteous violence is a divine duty when all peaceful means of preserving justice have failed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Derives its name and trickster/messenger function from the Yoruba Eshu, but is heavily recontextualized as a collective of human spirits policing karma.
GOLD SEAM: Dynamic, martial lords of thunder and lightning whose violent power commands deep regional reverence.
GOLD SEAM: Shares the exact West African origin as the Yoruba gatekeeper, retaining the absolute monopoly over cosmic communication.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba lord of iron transplanted intact, though in Haiti he dons a general's uniform to fight the revolution.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fiery, airborne, thunder-wielding spirit who represents explosive justice and martial power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fierce spirits of iron, war, and aggressive healing through radical action.
Both are Southern/West African powers tied to storm and the sky's bounty, but Sango is a thunder-king and Nomkhubulwane a rain-and-grain princess — a loose regional rhyme, not a cognate.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): the 19th-century Yoruba Bible rendered Satan as 'Èṣù,' and Aladura demonology inherited the equation — the orisha of the crossroads recast as the Enemy the praying churches battle.

Neighbouring West African possession idioms — Ṣàngó mounts his elegun as the iskoki ride their girka-made 'mares'; Hausa and Yoruba are unrelated language families, so the kinship is regional-typological, never cognate.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Yoruba people of southwestern Nigeria, Benin, and Togo, and, through the Atlantic slave trade, the root of major New World religions (Cuban Santeria/Lucumi, Brazilian Candomble, Trinidadian Orisha). It venerates a remote supreme God, Olodumare (Olorun), through hundreds of orisha, deified powers and ancestors such as Shango, Ogun, and Eshu, and reads destiny through the Ifa divination system. It remains living in West Africa and across a vast diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: Olodumare is the transcendent creator and owner of all destinies, while the orisha are the active divine powers who govern nature, craft, and human affairs, many of them former kings or heroes elevated to divinity. Creation descends from the sacred city of Ile-Ife, where the orisha Obatala (or, in variant accounts, Oduduwa) formed dry land upon the primeval waters. Each person is born with an ori, an inner head or personal destiny chosen before birth; after death the good join the ancestors, and reincarnation within the lineage is widely expected.
dossier.practice: Worship proceeds through offering, drumming, dance, praise-poetry (oriki), and spirit-possession, in which an orisha 'mounts' a devoted priest or priestess; specialized priesthoods serve particular orisha and initiate their devotees. Above all, the babalawo ('father of secrets') consults Ifa, a corpus of 256 odu (signs) and their memorized verses, to diagnose problems and prescribe sacrifice (ebo). Eshu, keeper of the crossroads, is honored first so that offerings and messages reach their destination; ethics emphasize good character (iwa pele) and right relationship with ancestors and destiny.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition with no scripture; its principal 'text' is the immense memorized corpus of Ifa, the odu and their verses, together with praise-poems, myths, and proverbs, now also recorded by scholars such as Wande Abimbola and William Bascom.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds destiny and divination, a cosmos in which each life is a chosen fate to be read, negotiated, and set right through sacrifice, with the divine reached only through a required trickster-gatekeeper.
sources: E. Bolaji Idowu, 'Olodumare: God in Yoruba Belief' (1962)
William Bascom, 'Ifa Divination: Communication Between Gods and Men in West Africa' (1969)
Wande Abimbola, 'Ifa: An Exposition of Ifa Literary Corpus' (1976)
